title: From 1999: The Great Communicator
summary: Can an expressive young conductor convince a veteran symphony orchestra to play with all its heart?

summary: Can an expressive young conductor convince a veteran symphony orchestra to play with all its heart?

title: Debate rages over TV ads on wolf snaring
summary: Debate rages over TV ads on wolf snaring By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Backers of a ballot initiative to ban wolf snaring are calling on television stations to stop running advertisements by snaring proponents. The ads make false claims, they say. Meanwhile, one of their own advertisements has come under fire and won't be aired by one television station that found it "too dramatic." "My eyes welled up, I was right on the edge of crying, of having tears," said Al Bramstedt, general manager of KTUU Channel 2. The 30-second spot shows a snared wolf that has chewed off its paw, but is still alive, trapped and hopping around. Bramstedt said it is the first time he can remember nixing a campaign ad. Both sides of the wolf snaring issue devised strategies centered on intensive campaigni…

title: Lawmakers seek elusive solution
summary: Lawmakers seek elusive solution By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - The Legislature returned to work briefly in special session Thursday, the day after last-minute efforts towrite a long-range fiscal plan collapsed in deadlock between the House and Senate over the future of Permanent Fund. Gov. Tony Knowles and some lawmakers said they're as intent now as ever to find a new way to finance state government and lessen reliance on oil revenue. But crafting a plan that the House, Senate and Knowles can agree on is "almost an impossible hurdle," Sen. President Drue Pearce said Thursday. Pearce, R-Anchorage, said she won't let any plan out of the Senate unless it has support from 11 of its 15 Republicans. She predicted that will happen but couldn't say when. "We were not about t…

title: Kenai enjoys autumn run of tourists
summary: Kenai enjoys autumn run of tourists By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - Tourism on the Kenai Peninsula is supposed to die like a spawned-out salmon after Labor Day. But this year, the run of travelers wouldn't stop rolling down the highway, hotels and visitor centers say. Nearly 4,600 people stopped at the Soldotna Visitor Information Center during September, a 40 percent spike over the same month in 1997. "It is quite a jump," said Randi Smith, Soldotna Chamber of Commerce office manager. "More people are into the whole Alaska experience, not just fishing but sightseeing, fall foliage, the hunting, hiking, canoeing and rafting trips." Chambers of commerce across the Peninsula cite a mix of reasons for the lingering season. Several summers of roadwork have tamed the once n…

title: Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Speaking of language Backers diagram official English measure By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Not much would change in Alaska if voters adopt an initiative making English the state's official language, supporters told Native educators at a conference Thursday. Proponents of the measure don't intend it to. Instead, the chairman of a national group pushing the initiative said, the measure is simply part of a broader campaign to make English the nation's official language. "We're not doing this because it's Alaska," said Mauro Mujica, chairman of the Washington, D.C.-based group U.S. English. "We're doing it because we have to do it in every state. It's not only about Alaska. You're part of America here." Mujica said 24 states have passed laws declaring English as the official langua…

title: Latest plan pays state costs before dividends
summary: Latest plan pays state costs before dividends By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press Alaska State Legislature web site See related pages: http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - A group of lawmakers is backing a proposal to use the earnings of the Alaska Permanent Fund to pay for government first and dividends second, which would cause a steep decline in the annual checks to Alaskans. The latest proposal for solving the state's financial problems would create the Alaska Income Account. Each year, the Permanent Fund would deposit 5.75 percent of its market value into the new fund, which would then be combined with other income sources and used to balance the state's budget. It would then pay out the Permanent Fund dividends. "This is a way to absolutely protect state spending and state growth a…

title: Golfers hit links in Unalaska
summary: Golfers hit links in Unalaska Participants in the Unalaska Tundra Classic Golf Tournament play the eighth hole on Sunday. The nine-hole event was held Saturday and Sunday by the Unalaska/Dutch Harbor Chamber of Commerce to raise money for scholarships. The players are, from left, Michael Tutiakoff, Patrick Dushkin, Nick McDonald, P.J. Dushkin and Ray Magpantay. (Jim Paulin / Special to the Anchorage Daily News) Back | Top | Home | User Agreement Problems? Suggestions? Let us hear from you. Copyright © 1998 The Anchorage Daily News

title: Flamboyant lawyer to leave Alaska
summary: Flamboyant lawyer to leave Alaska By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter Eighty years old and plagued with ill health, attorney Edgar Paul Boyko, who fanned Alaska law and politics into 40 years of public pyrotechnics, is liquidating his law practice and moving to the Seattle area. Boyko said he and his wife, Georgie, will leave Alaska when the case he is working on wraps up. Boyko had three small strokes in May but "made a lot of progress" with rehabilitation, he said. Then he had a second series of strokes in July and "lost a lot of ground." He is paralyzed on his left side, he said, and often suffers from vertigo. His eyesight is all but gone. A government lawyer from Washington, D.C., Boyko arrived in Alaska in 1952 as the attorney for the Bureau of Land Management. He left that job the …

title: Landowners gain backing from Young
summary: Landowners gain backing from Young Bill seeks payment for sites seized under conservation law By JOHN HUGHES The Associated Press WASHINGTON - Congressional Republicans are renewing their effort to cushion the impact of the Endangered Species Act on private landowners - this time by requiring the government to pay landowners who are forced to keep their land dormant because of the law. A bill written by Rep. Don Young, R-Alaska, and co-sponsored by more than 25 House Republicans equates the act's effect on private land as a "taking" of the land for public use. The measure would prevent such a taking unless the government obtains landowner permission, negotiates an agreement or pays compensation. The latest proposal, introduced last month, is another signal that the Republicans haven't give…

title: Frozen Lost Lake could lose to Peninsula's brutal winter
summary: Frozen Lost Lake could lose to Peninsula's brutal winter By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor Almost 2,000 feet high in the Kenai Mountains, Lost Lake barely emerged from winter's embrace late last summer, and now there is speculation that the lake once known for its big rainbow trout might remain locked in ice through this year. The trout, it should be noted, are history now. They became victims of what biologists call winter kill - the gradual depletion of oxygen beneath the ice of a lake frozen too long. As for this lake, no one knows what the future holds. "It wasn't snow-free until the end of July last year," said Irene Lindquist of the U.S. Forest Service in Seward, and "we think there's more snow there now than there was last year." Couple unusually deep snow with National Wea…

title: Alyeska workers may end century watching valves
summary: Alyeska workers may end century watching valves By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Some Alyeska Pipeline Service Co. workers may spend New Year's Eve of the New Millennium parked in the dark along some forlorn stretch of the North Slope haul road. They would be part of the company's contingency plan to make sure the "Y2K" computer bug does not cause any pump or valve failures along the trans-Alaska pipeline when computer clocks turn over at the end of the year, Alyeska president Bob Malone told a Senate panel on Thursday. Bob Malone told the Senate Special Committee on the Year 2000 Technology Problem that the company will station workers in key areas along the pipeline. "We are mobilizing our incident command system, which is a well-oiled team of people who handle …

title: Hospital seeks re-vote
summary: Hospital seeks re-vote Nurses reject deal, seek negotiations By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Under increasing public pressure to end the 8-day-old nurses strike, Providence Alaska Medical Center officials said Thursday they want the union to take another vote on the hospital's last offer, and in the meantime they will give nurses raises equal to what was on the table. Most nurses at the Anchorage hospital are on strike, so the pay raise will benefit only those who cross the picket line. It takes effect Sunday, officials said. Though nurses voted overwhelmingly last week to reject that contract and strike, Providence administrator Gene O'Hara said the hospital was uncertain whether all the nurses saw the offer and knew the particulars. The hospital on Thursday mailed copies of the contrac…

title: Study casts doubt on seafood worries
summary: Study casts doubt on seafood worries By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter For years, Alaskans who eat a lot of whale, seal and fish have heard conflicting reports on whether they should worry about the low levels of mercury found in their subsistence food. The toxic form of mercury, called methylmercury, can cause birth defects. State and federal public health officials disagree on how much of it is safe to consume. The Environmental Protection Agency is pushing for stricter guidelines. It wants to lower the amount of methylmercury that is safe. State health officials worry that will trigger fish consumption advisories suggesting people not eat more than 4 ounces of fish a week. The state argues that the nutritional benefits of seafood in the diet must be weighed against the possibility…

title: House ups state's share of leases
summary: House ups state's share of leases Legislation halves percentage of royalties deposited into Permanent Fund By DAN JOLING The Associated Press Alaska State Legislature See related pages: http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - A bill reducing the percentage of mineral and petroleum lease earnings deposited into the Permanent Fund was approved by the House on Thursday. The House also voted to define how Alaskans are counted for redrawing election districts, increase penalties for manufacturing the stimulant methamphetamine, and grant limited immunity to the state and municipalities from lawsuits stemming from year 2000 computer system failures. Rep. Norm Rokeberg, R-Anchorage, sponsored House Bill 96, which would lower the amount deposited into the Permanent Fund from royalties on new leases.…

title: Bartlett grad led Colorado students to safety
summary: Bartlett grad led Colorado students to safety By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Violence Policy Center -- Fact Sheet on Colorado School Shooting, CDC Fact Sheet on School Violence, Index of School Violence Links See related pages: http://www.vpc.org/press/9904col.htm http://www.cdc.gov/od/oc/media/fact/violence.htm http://7-12educators.tqn.com/msub67schoolsafety.htm A Bartlett High School graduate led 33 frightened students to safety Tuesday when two classmates went on a killing rampage in Colorado. Jason Webb, who was born in Anchorage and graduated from Bartlett in 1988, teaches English at Columbine High School in Littleton and coaches its baseball team. His name initially puzzled an Anchorage woman, Diana Leach, who received this voice message Wednesday morning: "Yes, Jason's OK, and, …

title: Army backs BP on sea pipeline
summary: Army backs BP on sea pipeline By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers' staff has sided with BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. over the routing of the first undersea oil pipeline to be constructed on the North Slope. Issuance of a construction permit for the oil company's Northstar project, however, has been delayed while the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service decides whether it will appeal. If the permit decision drags on past Friday, BP said Wednesday, it is likely to scrub plans to begin construction of Seal Island, where the Northstar prospect would be developed. A ice road has been built to the shoal, and the company had hoped to haul up to 700,000 cubic yards of gravel during the winter construction season to build it up to a 5-acre island. "…

title: Stevens, Babbitt seek agreement on Izembek road
summary: Stevens, Babbitt seek agreement on Izembek road By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Sen. Ted Stevens is trying to reach a deal with Interior Secretary Bruce Babbitt that would end a looming confrontation over a proposed road through the Izembek National Wildlife Refuge. Stevens, chairman of the Senate Appropriations Committee, added $700,000 to a 1999 transportation spending bill Thursday night that would finance a U.S. Army Corps of Engineers study of "rural access issues in Alaska." If a deal can be worked out between Stevens and Babbitt, however, it's virtually certain the study would focus on alternatives to the proposed King Cove road, perhaps including a causeway over Native-owned lands that would avoid most or all of the refuge. The road is sought by the state…

title: Diverse support aided parks bonds
summary: Diverse support aided parks bonds School measures pass by narrow margin By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter ADN ElectionCentral and Complete Election Results See related pages: http://www.adn.com/elections/99muni/index.html http://www4.alaskanet.com/elections99/results.ami Advocates for the $6 million parks bond proposal pulled off the unexpected Tuesday - approval of bonds that often fail in Anchorage - due mainly to hard work by an unusual collection of people, from parents of softball players to road engineers, say post-election analysts. Many also credited Mayor Rick Mystrom, who suffered a defeat of similar bonds last year. This time, Mystrom jumped in early and called together a group of football and soccer coaches, hockey families, road builders and trail supporters for a meet…

title: Arco-BP merger likely to cost Alaska more jobs
summary: Arco-BP merger likely to cost Alaska more jobs By EVE ROSE Daily News reporter BP Amoco and Arco web sites See related pages: http://www.bpamoco.com/ http://www.arco.com/ A proposed multibillion-dollar takeover of Atlantic Richfield Co. by BP Amoco likely would spur another round of job cuts in Alaska's oil industry as the two companies seek to avoid duplication, economists and other analysts said Monday. It was unclear just how many of the 2,250 Arco and BP jobs in Alaska would be at stake. And officials of the two oil companies in the state declined comment Monday on potential job losses. But several analysts said they expect the losses could be significant. If history is any guide, the companies could be expected to cut between 10,000 to 12,000 jobs worldwide. Most of the cuts probably …

title: 'The mountain was screaming'
summary: 'The mountain was screaming' Snowmobilers missed avalanche clues; new 'supermachines' also blamed By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor Only the avalanche-savvy suspected danger as the first Sunday of spring dawned sunny and marvelously inviting in Turnagain Pass a week ago. On the slopes of Tincan Peak, southeast of the Seward Highway, telemark skiers slipped climbing skins onto their skis and followed the snowshoe tracks of snowboarders up into the Kenai Mountains. In the pass, snowmobilers began unloading in the parking lot of the Chugach National Forest and fanning out through upper Granite Creek valley. No one could guess that by nightfall six people would be dead beneath a mountain of snow. "It was a gorgeous day," skier Brian Miskill said. "It was a busy day. There were people …

title: New avalanche fears end search
summary: New avalanche fears end search By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter Moving pictures, Colorado Avalanche Information Center, Cyberspace Snow and Avalanche Center, Westwide Avalanche Network See related pages: http://www.adn.com/movie/avalanche1.mov http://www.adn.com/movie/avalanche2.mov http://www.caic.state.co.us/ http://www.csac.org/ http://www.avalanche.org/ More than a foot of snow fell overnight in Turnagain Pass, raising concerns Thursday about the prospect of new slides and leading Alaska State Troopers to indefinitely suspend the search for the sixth victim of Sunday's deadly avalanche. Hundreds of volunteers and National Guardsmen directed by avalanche experts had recovered the bodies of five of the missing snowmobilers by Wednesday evening. title: Attorneys general ask Exxon to pay up for spill
summary: Attorneys general ask Exxon to pay up for spill By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau Hard Aground, Exxon Valdez: Legacy of a Spill, Legacy of an Oil Spill: 10 Years after the Exxon Valdez Symposium See related pages: http://www.adn.com/evos/ http://www.adn.com/evos/evos.html http://www.oilspill.state.ak.us/10year.htm JUNEAU - The Knowles administration and attorneys general from three dozen states and territories Thursday urged Exxon Corp. to pay a $5 billion court-ordered judgment for damages caused by the 1989 Exxon Valdez oil spill. Exxon responded Thursday that it believes the punitive damage verdict is "unjust and excessive" and plans to keep fighting it. The National Association of Attorneys General delivered a letter to Exxon chairman Lee Raymond saying Exxon should stop appe…

title: See related stories from:
summary: Fifth victim found Palmer man still missing By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter Website: Colorado Avalanche Information Center, Cyberspace Snow and Avalanche Center, Westwide Avalanche Network See related pages: http://www.adn.com/movie/avalanche1.mov http://www.adn.com/movie/avalanche2.mov http://www.caic.state.co.us/ http://www.csac.org/ http://www.avalanche.org/ TURNAGAIN PASS - A fifth victim of Sunday's deadly Turnagain Pass avalanche was found late Wednesday afternoon and the search was to resume today for a sixth man believed to have been trapped high on the slope. The body of Air Force Tech Sgt. Victor Jones was discovered shortly before 6:30 p.m., about an hour after searchers uncovered his snowmachine. Alaska State Troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said Jones' snowmachine was under…

title: Missing tally now down to 2
summary: Missing tally now down to 2 Turnagain searchers to return to slide today By DOUG O'HARRA Daily News reporter Moving pictures features, Colorado Avalanche Information Center web site, Cyberspace Snow and Avalanche Center web site, Westwide Avalanche Network web site See related pages: http://www.adn.com/movie/avalanche1.mov http://www.adn.com/movie/avalanche2.mov http://www.caic.state.co.us/ http://www.csac.org/ http://www.avalanche.org/ TURNAGAIN PASS - Under a broken sky still spitting wet snow, an army of nearly 200 volunteers probed steep slopes and deep, dense snow Tuesday in a stubborn search for the two remaining victims of Sunday's avalanche that killed at least four snowmachiners. Despite thousands of searcher hours and the use of high-tech military metal detectors, Tech Sgt. Victo…

title: Oil critics demand safer tankers
summary: Oil critics demand safer tankers By MAUREEN CLARK The Associated Press Other stories in this series and other related stories on this topic. See related pages: http://www.adn.com/evos/evos.html http://www.adn.com/evos/ VALDEZ - Oil industry representatives and government regulators gathered here Sunday to mark the progress made in preventing oil spills in the decade since the Exxon Valdez ran aground. But a group of industry critics said those efforts have focused too much on cleaning up spills and not enough on preventing them. "Specifically, we're calling for double-hull tankers," said Jim Sykes, executive director of Oilwatch Alaska, an industry watchdog group. It was March 24, 1989, when the tanker Exxon Valdez ran onto Bligh Reef, gashing its hull and spilling 11 million gallons of oi…

title: Heavy snowfall derails ski-train schedule
summary: Heavy snowfall derails ski-train schedule By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor Six feet of snow has stopped the Saturday ski train. The Nordic Ski Association of Anchorage announced Thursday that the run to Grandview in the Kenai Mountains - the first of the spring's two popular ski trains - is off until April 10. The fate of the March 27 ski train will be decided next week. Alaska Railroad officials earlier in the week notified the ski club that the popular trip was a no-go because of weather, said Kate Tomasi, 1999 ski-train coordinator. Railroad officials are worried about the risks of avalanche. One of those unexpectedly roared down the slopes of Alyeska Resort on Friday. Several skiers were caught, but no one was seriously injured. Railroad officials are worried about similar sl…

title: Mentally ill fill cells
summary: Mentally ill fill cells New program will treat, not jail, minor offenders By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter The mentally ill are pouring into jails and prisons, and Alaska judges, lawyers and health professionals say what's happening to them is inhumane and must end. Their disease plays out in public. The disheveled man at Denny's who screams at the cashier after he's caught trying to slip out without paying. The panhandler chasing down a tourist who said no. The psychotic woman who hallucinates in Nordstrom. Their illness out of control, they create a scene. So they are arrested, many for minor crimes like trespassing, disorderly conduct or misdemeanor assault, say members of a criminal justice group addressing the problem. "We are not talking about inherently dangerous people. We are tal…

title: Harrowing rescue saves crabbing crew
summary: Harrowing rescue saves crabbing crew By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter Thorne Tasker, captain of a crab boat going down in the Bering Sea, was up on the mast, hanging by his feet like a man on a trapeze. He had hooked his ankles around the railing at the edge of a catwalk and was stretching himself down a steel cable. Just out of reach was the weighted end of a 200-foot rescue rope, snagged in the rigging. At the far end of the rope was a Coast Guard crewman inside a helicopter that was trying to hold its position in a screaming winter gale. The wind was blowing the weights - three 5-pound sandbags - straight out like flags. The boat was wallowing in heavy seas. Twenty feet above the wheelhouse, 60 feet above the swells, Tasker was swinging through the darkness like a metronome. But what …

title: Packed ice strands otters
summary: Packed ice strands otters Starvation, scavengers taking toll near Bristol Bay By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter See Related File Persistent winds out of the west and north and unusually cold temperatures have packed sea ice tight along the Bristol Bay coast of the Alaska Peninsula, effectively barricading hundreds of sea otters from open water and access to clam beds that are critical to their survival. Residents of Port Heiden and Chignik Lake report that some otters are pushing ashore, wandering into Port Heiden and apparently even attempting to cross over to the Pacific side of the peninsula. Many have starved and many more are likely to, villagers and state biologists say. "It's pretty grim," said Virginia Aleck of Chignik Lake. Aleck said she and her husband, Nick, recently traveled a…

title: See related stories from:
summary: Whalers offer change Feds hear proposal for new beluga hunting guidelines By TOM BELL Daily News reporter See Related File While federal officials ponder whether to list Cook Inlet's beluga whales as an endangered species, a group representing most of the Inlet's beluga hunters have proposed fundamentally changing the way they hunt. They said they want to ban commercial hunting and register all hunters. They want to require hunters to harpoon whales before shooting, so fewer would sink and be lost, and to hunt only in shallow waters, where they would be easier to recover. Their proposal comes as federal officials, tribal leaders and hunters begin hashing out some kind of agreement for regulating beluga hunting. Both sides say they want "co-management," which would involve various sides cre…

title: Voters push Assembly down moderate path
summary: Voters push Assembly down moderate path By DON HUNTER and ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporters ADN ElectionCentral and Complete Election Results See related pages: http://www.adn.com/elections/99muni/index.html http://www4.alaskanet.com/elections99/results.ami Fewer than one in five city voters made it to the polls Tuesday, but the ones who did elected an Anchorage Assembly more likely to be interested in environmental safeguards and commercial sprawl than in flashpoint social issues, say former and newly elected members. "It's actually a more moderate Assembly," said Mark Begich, a former three-term member from East Anchorage. His view was underscored by other former and sitting Assembly members. While more moderate on many issues, the new body will probably be more fiscally conservativ…

title: Fiscal forecast bright
summary: Fiscal forecast bright UAA study finds state funds stable By T.A. BADGER Daily News reporter A University of Alaska Anchorage economist said Monday that despite low oil prices now and possibly in the future, the state budget can be indefinitely funded at today's $2.3 billion level, with another $850 million set aside annually for Alaska Permanent Fund dividends. For Scott Goldsmith, one of the state's leading economists and author of a new state fiscal report, such stability is a far cry from the forecasts he made earlier in the decade. In 1992, he was pushing the idea that a fiscal gap was building within state spending that would lead to a budgetary day of reckoning. What has changed the state fortunes has been the rapid growth of its financial assets - to the point that they create more…

title: 'Truth' team rips Exxon over oil spill
summary: 'Truth' team rips Exxon over oil spill By DOUG O'HARRA Daily News reporter SEATTLE - Sitting at a table Monday with 20 jars of Exxon Valdez crude oil gathered last week from Sleepy Bay on Latouche Island, a group of Alaskans talked of dying fish and damaged lives. Under posters of a blackened otter and an oiled rock, they talked about a decade of broken promises to pay for damages and to protect the environment. They asked their listeners - including a couple of dozen delegates to an oil spill conference - to remember that Prince William Sound and many of its residents still don't feel whole. The message to Exxon Corp. couldn't be plainer, said Rick Steiner, an activist and biologist with the University of Alaska's Marine Advisory Program. "It's time to tell the truth and admit the extent …

title: Mass offered for victims
summary: Mass offered for victims The Catholic Archdiocese of Anchorage will offer a memorial Mass today for the victims of Tuesday's killings at a Colorado high school. Archbishop Francis T. Hurley will celebrate the Mass, and those in attendance will have the chance to sign a sympathy card for victims and their families. The card will be sent to Columbine High School in Littleton, Colo. The Mass begins at 12:10 p.m. at Holy Family Cathedral on Fifth Avenue downtown. Back | Top | Home | User Agreement Problems? Suggestions? Let us hear from you. title: Californians fear merger of BP-Arco
summary: Californians fear merger of BP-Arco By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - BP Amoco's proposed purchase of Atlantic Richfield Co. is facing opposition in California, where about half of the gasoline used by 24 million people is refined from Alaska North Slope crude oil. The combination of BP and Arco would give the resulting company such a lock on Alaska crude oil production that it could drive up gas prices in California, consumer lawyers in that state have argued to the Federal Trade Commission. Because of that, the commission should block the $26 billion acquisition, said Dan Mogin, principal of a law firm representing gasoline users in a class-action lawsuit that charges price fixing by California's major gasoline companies. "That concentration would undoubtedly lead to h…

title: Buchanan calls for 'pro-life' U.S.
summary: Buchanan calls for 'pro-life' U.S. By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter Presidential candidate Pat Buchanan capped a three-day campaign swing through Alaska with a brief spell at the pulpit Sunday, where he urged parishioners at the Anchorage Baptist Temple to take action in what he called America's deeply rooted cultural war. Bathed in the television ministry's camera lights and backed by a blue screen with images of puffy white clouds and a floating cross, the conservative talk-show host advocated the boycott of advertisers of "perverted" popular entertainment and called the anti-abortion movement "God's cause ... above all other today." Four of Buchanan's eight Alaska stops, in Kenai, Fairbanks and Anchorage, were at right-to-life fund-raisers. "We've got to make both parties pro-life, and …

title: Knowles challenges lawmakers
summary: Knowles challenges lawmakers Governor wants plan for subsistence issue By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau Alaska Governor's Office, Alaska State Legislature web sites See related pages: http://www.gov.state.ak.us/ http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Gov. Tony Knowles asked the Legislature again Monday to approve a constitutional amendment that could give rural residents the first rights to hunting and fishing for subsistence. But he held out little hope the lawmakers would endorse the measure. And he vowed to bring the Legislature back to work this summer or fall if lawmakers don't solve the subsistence problem by the end of its regular session next month. Without action, the federal government will take over subsistence fishing on federal waters Oct. 1. If lawmakers fail to ap…

title: U.S. avalanche experts reconsider disaster risks
summary: U.S. avalanche experts reconsider disaster risks By JOSEPH B. VERRENGIA The Associated Press It's a recipe for disaster, whether you live in Aspen or the Alps or Alaska. Combine deep snow with steep slopes and too many people, and in a few seconds a winter vacation playground can become a killing field, according to avalanche experts. Avalanches across central Europe have killed more than 70 people in what has become one of the century's snowiest and most precarious winters. In Austria, massive slides suddenly thundered into the picturesque villages of Galteur and Valzur recently. Thirty-eight people were killed. The calamities in Europe are prompting U.S. officials to consider whether similar disasters could happen here. It has been a relatively dry winter, with the exception of the Pacif…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Avalanche dead found The Associated Press The bodies of two men killed in separate avalanches last week were located by searchers on Saturday, Alaska State Troopers said. The body of Kenneth Hayes, 44, of Healy was found about 10:30 a.m. in the debris of an avalanche that occurred Friday near Cantwell, troopers said. Hayes was one of five snowmachiners buried when a slide roared down a steep gully in a popular area 11 miles southeast of Cantwell. The other four people were rescued. Near Cordova, the body of Gary Stone, 46, was found pinned between his backhoe and logs that had been used as a temporary bridge at a hydroelectric project construction site. The Cordova man was running the backhoe Thursday in the steep canyon when a load of snow slid down a 2,000-foot slope and buried him. Stat…

title: Wilderness road project lambasted
summary: Wilderness road project lambasted Proposal would connect Cold Bay and King Cove By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - A proposed road through a wilderness area of a remote Alaska wildlife refuge is shaping up as one of the key environmental fights in the Congress this year. Alaska's Republican congressional delegation said that the road they are seeking through the Izembek National Wildlife Refuge would allow residents of King Cove to reach an all-weather airport in Cold Bay for evacuation to Anchorage during medical emergencies. "This is a matter not only of life and death, but of justice," said Rep. Don Young, chairman of the House Resources Committee. He was joined at a press conference by King Cove road advocates and by Alaska Sen. Frank Murkowski, chairman of the …

title: Clam diggers take their chances
summary: Clam diggers take their chances Low tides prompt state's annual warning of shellfish risks By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau Clam Gulch and Deep Creek area SOLDOTNA - When state officials tell die-hard clammers to dig at their own risk during this weekend's low tides, they aren't just talking about hip-deep snow blocking the beach. As Alaska enters another clamming season, it does so without any real public-beach testing program. Shellfish feed by filtering nutrients out of saltwater, which means the tender white clam meat that goes into fritters and chowder could also bear nasty bacteria or deadly toxins that trigger paralytic shellfish poisoning. Not that eating Clam Gulch and Deep Creek razor clams has ever hurt anybody. There's no record it has. But the state says it can't affo…

title: House whittles local aid
summary: House whittles local aid UAA, worker safety appeals fall short By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press Alaska State Legislature web site See related pages: http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Ignoring warnings that the cuts would gut many village governments, House Republicans rejected a bid to restore nearly $48 million in aid to local communities Monday. The proposed budget eliminates municipal assistance and revenue sharing, two programs that provide more than half of the budgets for many small towns. The Republican majority cut the two programs as part of its response to the $1.2 billion gap between state spending and revenue. "It's a big number," said Rep. Eldon Mulder, co-chairman of the House Finance Committee. "If it was only for the current year, it wouldn't be a problem, but it is…

title: More eyes size up BP-Arco deal
summary: More eyes size up BP-Arco deal By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau BP Amoco, Arco (Atlantic Richfield), Alaska State Legislature, and the Governor's Office web sites See related pages: http://www.bpamoco.com/ http://www.arco.com/ http://www.legis.state.ak.us/ http://www.gov.state.ak.us/ JUNEAU - Gov. Tony Knowles on Monday said he's directed two more top officials to study BP Amoco's proposed purchase of Atlantic Richfield Co. to protect Alaska's environment and economy. The Knowles administration also will hire oil industry and antitrust experts, and could spend $250,000 reviewing the acquisition, Attorney General Bruce Botelho said. "My bottom line with respect to this merger is very clear: making sure that the economic interests of Alaska families, jobs and business are protecte…

title: Stevens is fed up with subsistence
summary: Stevens is fed up with subsistence By DAN JOLING The Associated Press Senator Stevens web site See related pages: http://stevens.senate.gov/ JUNEAU - U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens' wide-ranging annual speech to the Legislature on Thursday was silent on one of the great federal issues facing Alaska: subsistence. But in response to a question from a freshman legislator, Stevens said he is bowing out of proposed solutions. "I butted my head against this wall eight years, maybe nine years, in a row," Stevens said. "I finally got the message. It's your decision, your judgment, and I think the state awaits your answer." Federal law passed in 1980 requires that rural residents have priority for hunting and fishing on federal lands. The law conflicts with the Alaska Constitution, which states that fish an…

title: Alaska AIDS cases drop in '98, but reports of hepatitis C rise
summary: Alaska AIDS cases drop in '98, but reports of hepatitis C rise By T.A. BADGER The Associated Press Alaska saw a significant decline in the number of new AIDS cases last year but experienced a big upswing in the number of people diagnosed with the hepatitus-C virus, according to the state Division of Public Health. Statewide, 25 new AIDS cases were reported in 1998, according to the state. That was down about 30 percent from 1997, when 35 new cases were reported. The Anchorage-Mat-Su region accounted for 18 of the new AIDS cases, down from 24 the previous year. Four cases were reported in the Interior, two in Southeast and one in the Gulf of Alaska region, which includes the Kenai Peninsula and Prince William Sound. For hepatitis C, the state Section of Epidemiology recorded 1,004 newly dia…

title: Census drops plumbing question
summary: Census drops plumbing question Alaska officials say 2000 survey won't reflect 'honey bucket' figures By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - Alaska is about to lose its only statewide source of information on a problem still facing thousands of homes in rural communities: the lack of modern water and toilet systems. The U.S. Census Bureau has dropped two questions from the 2000 census, asking people in Alaska and the rest of the nation where they get their drinking water and whether their homes are connected to a public sewer system. The bureau asked the questions in the 1990 census but has now determined those questions are not mandated by law and there are no federal programs whose funding depended on the answers they provided. The decision will leave Alaska with no accurate…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Ship finds shelter Tanker stops leaking crude By PETER PORCO Daily News reporter A tanker that leaked crude oil in ice-choked Cook Inlet over the weekend was holing up at the mouth of Kachemak Bay Monday, out of the wind and out of further trouble, officials said. The estimated 420 gallons of oil spilled 15 miles west of Homer Saturday morning by the Chesapeake Trader has apparently evaporated or dispersed, and no shoreline has been soiled nor any wildlife harmed, said Brad Hahn, Southcentral spill response manager for the state Department of Environmental Conservation. "We've moved from a threatening situation to one not posing any threat," Hahn said. "We have a damaged vessel, not a leaking vessel." One watchdog group, the Cook Inlet Regional Citizens Advisory Council, agreed that the sp…

title: Court asked to halt Inlet lease sale
summary: Court asked to halt Inlet lease sale Environmentalists argue oil, gas development would leave belugas vulnerable By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau Online Pathfinder for Beluga Whales SOLDOTNA - Seven environmental groups have asked the state Superior Court to block this month's areawide Cook Inlet oil and gas lease sale unless changes are made to protect beluga whales. "We're ready to stop the entire lease sale until the state adequately considers the issue of beluga habitat," said Bob Shavelson, director of Cook Inlet Keeper. The 4.2-million acre lease sale, set for April 21, encompasses the Cook Inlet basin from Houston to Anchor Point, spanning the east and west shores. The National Marine Fisheries Service last summer asked the state not to lease certain tracts where the whales…

title: Teen shot in liquor store holdup
summary: Teen shot in liquor store holdup By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter For the second time in three months, a robber pointed a gun at Douglas Barnett's wife in the couple's Eagle River Liquor store. Barnett was calm and cool because he knew what he had to do. Barnett grabbed for the gun. It discharged, blasting a silver-dollar-size hole in the counter top. As Barnett and the robber struggled for the gun, Barnett's wife shot and wounded the robber with a handgun stored behind the counter. "He came in and jeopardized our freedom," Barnett said. "He wanted something that wasn't his." Kasey Turner, 18, was arrested after he walked into a fire station Monday seeking help for a wound he said resulted from a hunting accident, Anchorage police said. Turner, of Eagle River, was treated at Alaska Regiona…

title: Pipeline safety doubted
summary: Pipeline safety doubted System overseer says critics' claims not new By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Seven current and former managers for the trans-Alaska oil pipeline and Valdez tanker port are questioning whether the system that carries 20 percent of the nation's oil production is safe. "I feel - as do many other employees and former employees - that a disaster will occur," wrote James D. Whitaker, a former inspection supervisor in Valdez, in a letter forwarded over the weekend by former Virginia oil broker Chuck Hamel to government officials and John Browne, chief executive of London-based BP Amoco. The six others, who described themselves as midlevel managers on the pipeline, said in a separate letter last week that they had no confidence that Anchorage-base…

title: 'Madhouse' fishery a frantic flop
summary: 'Madhouse' fishery a frantic flop By WESLEY LOY Daily News reporter ABOARD THE LADY KONA - Ernest Demoski was lucky to get out of there with only a scratch on his boat. And his net intact. And his crew safe and sound. If only he'd come away with more fish. Those are the breaks in one of the wildest fishing spectacles in Bristol Bay, the Super Bowl of Alaska's commercial salmon fisheries and a place never known for leisure fishing. The Alaska Department of Fish and Game calls the spectacle the Naknek River "special harvest." Fishermen call it especially annoying. The Naknek is one of several major rivers draining into Bristol Bay. The special harvest there occurs rarely, when too few fish swim past the Naknek to spawn a new generation in the Kvichak River drainage farther up the bay. To giv…

title: Developer sees gold in city's eyesores
summary: Developer sees gold in city's eyesores By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Marc Marlow has so much energy, he bounds up stairs to the roofs of the run-down buildings he owns. The Anchorage developer recently stepped over dead pigeons and mittens discarded by the homeless to dash up a dark stairwell in the MacKay Building. Bursting onto the roof into sunshine, he gazed west. "Look at that," Marlow said, motioning toward another hulking eyesore, the old pea-soup-green Knik Arm Power Plant along Ship Creek. "It's going to be so cool." A few weeks before, Marlow had raced in his cowboy boots up a tall metal staircase inside the dormant power plant. From there, he spied the pink two-tone MacKay tower and discussed his plans for the two derelict buildings. With the help of a city property t…

title: Rush of reds fades to blah
summary: Rush of reds fades to blah By WESLEY LOY Daily News reporter NAKNEK - The excitement of a pre-Fourth of July explosion of red salmon in Bristol Bay was fading fast Friday as commercial fishermen began to catch a distinct whiff of mediocrity intruding on the fun. "We don't expect anything big anymore. It's too late," said Mike Boykov, a Los Angeles skipper patching a gillnet on his dock-bound boat, the Sofia. The 25-year Bristol Bay veteran said he delivered only about 1,000 pounds of fish Friday, way off the 6,200 he bagged July 1. After horrible harvests the past two summers, many people here were hoping for an unexpected bonanza after the first two days of the month saw fishermen haul in 6.5 million reds, the most taken ever in a 48-hour period at Bristol Bay, the state Department of Fis…

title: State Y2K fix priced at $19.3 million
summary: State Y2K fix priced at $19.3 million By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press JUNEAU - The Knowles administration Friday asked the Legislature for $19.3 million to fix computer problems that may be created by the coming of the new millennium. The state has identified 89 critical functions of government that could be affected by the problem, including transportation, prisons, courts, retirement and benefits programs, and Medicaid. The money requests range from $4.5 million to review transportation facilities, including airports and highway traffic lights, to $110,000 to examine the systems aboard the Kennicott, the state's brand-new $86 million ferry. "This happens in the middle of an Alaska winter," said Bob Poe, commissioner of administration. "If welfare checks don't get out, people could be…

title: Bog parcel might be added to Anchorage park
summary: Bog parcel might be added to Anchorage park By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter The Swamp Rats are willing to sell one of the last remaining private parcels of land within Connors Bog, and the city's Heritage Land Bank wants to buy it. Stumpy Faulkner, head of the Rats, a real estate investment group, said he will sell nine acres of bog land to the city for $45,000. The land would become part of a city park at the bog, assuming the Heritage Land Bank Advisory Commission and the Anchorage Assembly approve the deal later this summer. No opposition is expected, said Larry Houle, director of the Heritage Land Bank, the city's real estate office. "If you assemble this with the rest of Connors Bog, it really completes the parcel," he said. "And the price is right." The 217-acre Connors Lake…

title: Jury's verdict mixed on bear kill
summary: Jury's verdict mixed on bear kill By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter A Superior Court jury returned a mixed verdict Thursday for an Anchorage hunting guide on trial for killing a grizzly sow with a newborn cub. James Baum was acquitted of killing the sow but convicted of illegally possessing or transporting bear parts - the sow's hide and gall bladder. The jury hung on three other misdemeanor charges, including hunting on the same day as flying in an airplane. Prosecutor Sam Adams said he will retry the case. Baum, 57, declined to comment. The case against Baum grew out of a 1998 sting. An undercover agent, Joe Knarr, posed as a hunter and booked a $7,000 trip with Baum. Knarr, a Montana game warden on loan to the Alaska State Troopers, testified that he fired at the bear after Baum instruc…

title: Greenpeace objects to BP road
summary: Greenpeace objects to BP road The Associated Press The environmental group Greenpeace has asked a state court to stop BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. from building eight miles of ice roads to its Northstar oil field in the Beaufort Sea. Greenpeace's filings in Anchorage Superior Court claim the state Department of Natural Resources is allowing BP to go ahead with the ice road construction without proper permits. BP denies the accusation and says it has taken care of the paperwork needed to begin construction for the 145-million-barrel field. The oil company began building the ice road from the Kuparuk River Delta to Seal Island on Jan. 6, BP spokeswoman Carla Beam said. The ice road is to be used to haul gravel to the island, which is being enlarged to act as a base for the Northstar field. B…

title: Peninsula proves itself
summary: Peninsula proves itself Arco's module project succeeds By TONY LEWIS Daily News Reporter SOLDOTNA - Since moving to the Kenai Peninsula from Fairbanks in 1994, Mark Felz has paid the winter bills by driving a taxi, piecing together construction work or tinkering at odd jobs. Summers weren't bad skippering a halibut charter boat out of Homer. But he didn't want to endure another winter without a full-time job. He faced leaving his family temporarily to find better work in Seattle. Then the 44-year-old Kasilof man took a gamble. At the end of July, he walked off the halibut boat and enrolled at Kenai Peninsula College, where he learned to use a cutting torch, bevel steel and identify different kinds of pipe - skills needed by workers building oil-field modules in Nikiski for Arco Alaska Inc.…

title: Suspect takes his own life
summary: Suspect takes his own life By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter Anchorage police said a woman found slain in her Mountain View apartment Wednesday night was killed by a suicidal son who later decapitated himself with an elaborate noose he rigged in an isolated West Anchorage parking lot. Elizabeth B. Nicketa, 46, was found dead of stab wounds in the entryway of her second-floor apartment shortly before 9 p.m. Wednesday by a friend, police said. In a conspicuous spot in the home lay a note from one of her sons, police said. Jonathan M. Kyakwok, 26, wrote that he had killed his mother, that he wanted to die and that he wanted to go to Seattle. Police issued a notice to authorities across Anchorage to immediately locate Kyakwok and were distributing his photograph to media Thursday morning when t…

title: Teens tackle Little Su
summary: Teens tackle Little Su Project to restore river's banks, improve fish habitat By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Daily News Mat-Su Bureau PALMER - Some major construction is under way on the Little Susitna River, but if all goes well, there'll be little trace of the work except, perhaps, a few more trees and the slight scent of coconut. The construction is a river restoration project aimed at improving fish habitat along a popular three-mile stretch of the river near Point MacKenzie. The stretch abuts the Little Susitna River Public Use Facility, an increasingly popular destination for anglers in recent years because of its easy accessibility and plentiful salmon runs. But the popularity has come with a price as once-vegetated banks have been trampled bare and boat wakes have eaten away at the remaining …

title: Big Lake collision hurts girl
summary: Big Lake collision hurts girl Pair were jumping wakes, witness says By DAVID HUNN Daily News reporter Two boaters smashed their personal watercraft into each other Wednesday on Big Lake, seriously injuring one of the riders, witnesses said. John Cancel, 20, was riding his Jet Ski-type boat alongside another personal watercraft, driven by a 16-year-old girl. Authorities did not identify the girl Thursday. Witnesses said the two were jumping each other's wakes when they collided. Paramedics transported the teenager to Valley Hospital just after 8 p.m., Mat-Su Borough emergency officials said. Handyman George Kudlock had seen the two boaters all afternoon from the dock of the Klondike Inn, on the lake's shore. The two watercraft were almost alone on the lake, and were skidding across the wate…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Crews gaining ground City's haze eases; health alert still on By DON HUNTER Daily News reporter Firefighters controlled a blaze that threatened homes near Houston early Tuesday and continued to attack a 75-acre wildfire that is expected to burn through the week near a transmission line that links Anchorage to much of its power supply. Those fires were two of at least 48 burning across Alaska, state fire officials said. Many were caused by lightning strikes, but officials said some may have been started by fireworks over the holiday weekend. Smoke from more than a dozen Interior fires that shrouded Anchorage Sunday and Monday began to dissipate Tuesday, but city officials extended a health alert and advised people with heart and lung problems to remain indoors if possible. Most of the fires…

title: Neighbors help needy neighbors
summary: Neighbors help needy neighbors Aid to struggling Russians continues throughout state By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter In the city of Magadan, a young man living in an institution for disabled and elderly adults couldn't believe his good fortune one day last week when he was given a used jacket, bearing the insignia of some football team in San Francisco. "A real American coat!" he exclaimed to a priest, Michael Shields, who recounted the story. The garment came out of about 1,000 boxes of coats, gloves, hats and sweaters donated by people in Anchorage just before Christmas to help the cold and hungry people of Magadan. For several days last week, Shields, a 1968 West High graduate, and Sergei Talanov, head of the Anchorage-based Russian American Business Education Center, distributed the …

title: 75 years penalty for girl's death
summary: 75 years penalty for girl's death By LIZ RUSKIN Daily News reporter Until an ambulance was called to the house on Queen Victoria Drive, Marcias and Michael Reinhold seemed like good foster parents, especially to 2-year-old Janessa Aguirre, whom they hoped to adopt. But then, at lunch time on Dec. 15, 1997, Marcias Reinhold called 911 and said there had been an accident in the bathtub and Janessa wasn't breathing. Paramedics came to the house and rushed Janessa to the hospital where, despite massive life-support efforts, the little girl died of a head injury. Right away, the authorities noticed Marcias' story didn't add up. She said she found the baby face down in the water, but the girl's skin and diaper were dry. And there were burn marks on the child's face, at least eight round ones tha…

title: Missing woman's body found in Kincaid
summary: Missing woman's body found in Kincaid Kincaid Park body is missing woman By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter Nearly two weeks after their daughter disappeared, George and Mary Lou Hollett got a call from Anchorage police asking for her dental records. A man walking his dog in Kincaid Park, police told them, had found a woman's body in a ditch Monday night. On Tuesday, police confirmed the body was that of their daughter, Susan Bailey, a 43-year-old administrative assistant in Anchorage. They said she had been slain and that she was found without clothes. Police told them nothing else, George Hollett said. No arrests had been made Tuesday evening, and police wouldn't say if they had suspects. Police spokesmen, who made a brief public statement Tuesday, would not discuss a cause of death and sa…

title: State chops student funds
summary: State chops student funds Charter school appeals ruling By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter The state Department of Education has decided Anchorage's popular and innovative Family Partnership Charter School fits into the same category as correspondence schools. That means the state will give it nearly 40 percent less money per student next year, or $2.1 million instead of $3.4 million. Both the Anchorage School District and the charter school are appealing the ruling, saying the charter is more than a correspondence school and the loss of money will damage the program. "Of all the charter schools in Anchorage, the Family Partnership Charter School has stood out as the most creative and 'outside the box' charter school and most in line with the intent and vision of the charter school …

title: Salmon keep on coming
summary: Salmon keep on coming Bristol Bay likely to see 40 million The Associated Press KING SALMON - Red salmon continued to flow into Bristol Bay as fast as processors could handle them over the weekend, and state fish managers were staggering fishing periods to keep the catch from overwhelming the plants. "There's still a bit of a backlog," said James Brady, regional supervisor for the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. "We paused (Sunday) and that calmed things down a bit." Still, fishermen caught almost 1.3 million fish Sunday, about the capacity of the local processors. "Capacity is what is regulating how many fish are allowed into the streams," Brady said. "We're trying to fish shorter, more frequent openings to regulate the amount of fish available to the processing companies." State biol…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Greely given a plug Stevens likes fort for missile system The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - Fort Greely has the inside track to house the silos for a missile defense system if the facility ends up based in Alaska, said U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens, who toured the fort Monday. "It's apparent now that it's either Clear or Fort Greely," Stevens said Sunday at a press conference in Fairbanks. "And I believe that more and more it's tilting towards Fort Greely for the final deployment site for national missile defense - if it comes to Alaska." A missile base at Fort Greely, near Delta Junction, wouldn't interfere with plans for a private prison there, Stevens said. The military is studying plans for basing an anti-ballistic missile system in North Dakota, Alaska or perhaps both. A decision on whether to…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Slamming the door New law unleashed Draconian INS, critics say By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News Reporter More than 800 illegal immigrants have been rounded up in Alaska for deportation during the past two years as part of a nationwide crackdown. Many were snagged during raids at restaurants. Some were found in fish processing plants and hotels with counterfeit work documents. Some had overstayed their tourist visas. Some were drug dealers. Most had been here less than a year. But a number of them had set roots. They had been here long enough to create families. Others were here legally, having fled homelands for religious or political reasons, only to get sideways with the law here. They included a 25-year-old Laotian man who arrived here at age 6 and doesn't speak Laotian, a Mexican who liv…

title: Haze holds city in smoggy grip
summary: Haze holds city in smoggy grip By LARRY CAMPBELL Daily News reporter Mat-Su fires add to Interior's toll Smoke from more than a dozen fires in the Interior and the Matanuska-Susitna valleys enveloped the Anchorage Bowl in a thick haze Monday that hung like a curtain to obscure the surrounding landscape. Winds that were predicted Sunday to clear out the bowl never materialized, and two new fires in the Valley only intensified the haze that produced a brilliant orange sunset Sunday night. The haze caused city health officials Monday to issue a health advisory for residents with respiratory problems. The city's two major hospitals reported no increase in respiratory emergencies, however. The Interior fires east of Fort Yukon and near Chicken at the Canadian border were only being fought in ar…

title: Alaska charts a warm year across state
summary: Alaska charts a warm year across state Temperature's rising, Alaska figures show By SHEILA TOOMEY Daily News reporter With Anchorage thermometers reading zero by flashlight on a post-holiday Monday morning it was hard to worry about the world heating up, but year-end temperature tallies indicate Alaska was unusually warm in 1998. Increases in annual average temperatures were noted in all six reporting stations monitored by the Alaska Climate Research Center, especially up on the North Slope. The average temperature in Barrow jumped well into double digits - from 9.4 degrees to 16.9 degrees. That's 7.5 degrees above normal, a huge jump in the world of meteorology. Odd happenings that may or may not be related to the warming have been observed, said Charlie Brower, director of wildlife manag…

title: Letters to the editor
summary: Letters to the editor Quit crying about curfew I was disturbed at the tone toward the curfew law in Isaac Schapira's "Cut the Curfew" column (Perfect World, July 1). The column was well written but cried out about how unfair the curfew is to minors. I have one question: What in the world do you guys have to do after 1 a.m., anyway? Nothing, unless you want to go to Carrs. Freedom comes with responsibility. How responsible is hanging out after 1 a.m. and then waking up sometime after noon? What's next? A column about junior-high students whining about the driver's license age infringing on their rights? I realize parents tell their children when they can be home, but, parents, don't you think that they should be in bed? Kids, you have to understand that laws aren't there to make it easier f…

title: Bering Sea mysteries are case of 'whatdunit'
summary: Bering Sea mysteries are case of 'whatdunit' By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News Reporter For the second year in a row, 1998 saw many unusual events in the Bering Sea. Extensive die-offs of seabirds. Rare blooms of algae. Low salmon runs. Warmer-than-usual ocean temperatures. Altered ocean currents and atmospheric conditions. An odd fungus outbreak in the northern fur seals on the Pribilof Islands. The changes have biologists and scientists perplexed. But the strange events may be the first visible signs of the greenhouse effect in the Arctic, according to a report prepared by scientists who attended a November conference in Seattle to discuss the changes. About 75 scientists from Alaska, Maryland, Washington, Oregon, Russia, Canada and Japan attended the conference to try to make sense of what…

title: Hacker gets time in prison
summary: Hacker gets time in prison Former airman downloaded porn By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News Reporter A former Elmendorf airman was sentenced to 10 months in prison Thursday for using his home computer to hack into the U.S. Air Force base's computer system and for downloading child pornography from the Internet. "I don't consider myself a criminal," 27-year-old Michael Scott Moody told District Court Judge James Singleton during his sentencing Thursday. "Honestly, at the time, I didn't consider it hacking. I thought of it more as a prank," he said. "I was curious to know if I could access the computer at work. Being a government computer, I considered it a challenge. It worked. I didn't meant to hurt no one." Assistant Attorney General Dan Cooper cautioned the judge that all computer hackers us…

title: Park strip to be runway for a day
summary: Park strip to be runway for a day By DAVID HUNN Daily News reporter Anchorage thinks pioneer aviator Noel Wien is important. So important that the city is turning Delaney Park Strip into an airstrip in commemoration of Wien's accomplishments. And the Federal Aviation Administration is waiving rules that would normally forbid such urban flying. In 1924, Noel Wien made the first trip from Anchorage to Fairbanks in a Hisso-Standard Biplane. Then he pioneered wilderness flying statewide, heralding an explosion in Alaska aviation. The first true Bush pilot, writers and historians say. "He proved the practicality of flying," Wien's son Richard said. "He became known as the dean of Alaska aviation." Tuesday morning, Wien's sons honor will that accomplishment. They will pilot a blue and yellow ope…

title: Knowles signs bill to ease teen drinking penalties
summary: Knowles signs bill to ease teen drinking penalties By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Thousands of Alaska young people who have lost their driver's licenses because of underage drinking under the controversial "Use it, Lose it" law will have a chance to get their privileges back early. A bill revamping the law, signed Wednesday by Gov. Tony Knowles, addresses some of the criticisms of the 1994 measure by reducing the amount of time licenses are revoked. But it leaves in place the most controversial aspect - that underage drinkers can lose their licenses even if they were nowhere near a car when they were caught with alcohol. Both the new and old versions provide for the state Division of Motor Vehicles to revoke the driver's licenses of people under age 21 who have been cited for consumptio…

title: Knowles names year 2000 computer bug czar to Cabinet post
summary: Knowles names year 2000 computer bug czar to Cabinet post By ROBERT KOWALSKI Daily News Juneau Bureau JUNEAU - A longtime state administrator who's worked for the Legislature and the administrations of several Alaska governors will take over as commissioner of the state Department of Administration next year, Gov. Tony Knowles said Wednesday. Knowles appointed Bob Poe, 44, to replace outgoing Commissioner Mark Boyer as head of the department. As administration commissioner, Poe will oversee state employee contracts and various agencies including the Alaska Public Offices Commission, the Pioneers' Homes, the Division of Motor Vehicles, and the Oil and Gas Conservation Commission. Poe currently is coordinator of the state's efforts to prepare for the so-called Y2K problem associated with the…

title: Beluga toxin tests surprise scientists
summary: Beluga toxin tests surprise scientists Inlet whales cleaner than those of Chukchi, Beaufort seas By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Chemical contaminant levels in the blubber of Cook Inlet beluga whales are lower than belugas from the Eastern Chukchi and Eastern Beaufort seas, according to new study results that took scientists by surprise. "We were expecting to find higher levels in the Cook Inlet belugas because they are so close to a populated area," said Peggy Krohn, manager of the environmental assessment program at the National Marine Fisheries Services' laboratory in Seattle. Belugas from the Beaufort Sea have the highest levels of the cancer-causing agents that the tests looked for, including DDT, mirex, other compounds used in insecticides and pesticides, and PCBs, a chemical…

title: City post offices extend hours to keep holiday mail flowing
summary: City post offices extend hours to keep holiday mail flowing The Associated Press On what was expected to be the year's busiest day for the U.S. Postal Service, mail handlers in Alaska worked Monday to sort and ship an estimated 357,000 letters, cards and packages. The flood of Christmas mail is part of a nationwide surge estimated to total 280 million cards and letters from 21 million customers for the day, and 16.7 billion for the entire holiday season, according to the U.S. Postal Service. That's an increase of about 3 percent from last year. In Alaska, Postal Service spokeswoman Nancy Cain Schmitt said Monday's load of outgoing mail included about 40,000 pounds of parcels. A typical day sees about 8,000 pounds of packages move through the postal system in Alaska, Schmitt said. Next Mond…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: APD meets 'Oprah' Police take calls on live cable TV By DAVID HUNN Daily News reporter On television tonight, as "Dateline NBC" spotlights the newest con man in Florida or murderer loose in Arkansas, Anchorage residents can instead tune to their own city cable channel - Channel 47 - and talk to their own police about their own problems. The Anchorage Police Department will air a new live TV show, "Our Part of Town," at 7 p.m. It allows concerned residents to call in and discuss their worries with the lieutenant in charge of their corner of the woods. Consider it a combination of "NYPD Blue" and "Oprah." Tonight's show will feature Lt. Walt Monegan from West Anchorage. Monegan, with a police detective and representative of the fire department, will field calls. Police public affairs supervi…

title: Searching for answers
summary: Searching for answers Family focuses on solving mystery of missing lawyer By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter Hugh Headrick awoke at 6:30 a.m. Saturday to reminders that his daughter Amy had vanished. Headrick, scheduled for a 10-day sailing vacation in the Caribbean, has been sleeping on the floor of her two-bedroom South Anchorage apartment so he won't for a moment forget. On the floor beside him sleeps Amy's boyfriend; on the sofa her stepmother; in the bed, her mother and sister. "I made a decision to set up our command post at the apartment," he said. "It's uncomfortable, but this way we're all involved. This is our whole focus." It has worked. Each waking soul adds another voice to the single conversation that occupies these cramped, high-ceilinged rooms: how today to look for Amy. The …

title: King study muscles in on fishing holes
summary: King study muscles in on fishing holes By JON LITTLE Daily News Peninsula Bureau SOLDOTNA - State biologists think they might have better luck counting the Kenai River's world-class king salmon in a spot between two of the river's hottest fishing holes. Today, they start the first of what could be two years of studies to find out if they're right. The research involves casting gillnets in a prime king salmon fishing area in four-day blocks over the rest of the summer so it probably will mean some inconvenience for anglers, said Debby Burwen, a state Department of Fish and Game biologist in charge of the sonar counter program. Gillnets cast from a drifting boat will haul in kings and reds along a 220-yard length of fast-moving water at river mile 13.2, right between Falling In Hole and Hone…

title: Tardy kings worry Yukon
summary: Tardy kings worry Yukon The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - With this year's Yukon River king salmon run already late, fishermen and biologists are wondering if the harvest will be as weak as last year's disastrous run. "Everybody is pretty nervous about it," said Tanana subsistence fisherman Bill Fliris, one of thousands of villagers along the Yukon and Tanana rivers who depend on the king salmon run to fill their smokehouses. Fliris said that if the run were on time, fishermen in Tanana would be catching a few fish by now. While some villagers have moved to their fish camps, "nobody's brought a king salmon into town yet," he said. Subsistence and commercial fishermen have been warned that this year's run could be similar to last year's, when an estimated 122,000 kings returned to the Yukon.…

title: High tide helps tugboats refloat grounded cruise ship in Glacier Bay inlet
summary: High tide helps tugboats refloat grounded cruise ship in Glacier Bay inlet By PAUL QUEARY The Associated Press JUNEAU - Taking advantage of a helpful high tide, tugboats gingerly pulled a grounded cruise ship free Wednesday from the rocks that had held it fast for four days in a remote inlet in Glacier Bay National Park and Preserve. The Coast Guard anchored the 156-foot Wilderness Adventurer a few yards away from the rock. It began a detailed inspection of its hull and fuel tanks to decide whether to unload 4,300 gallons of fuel before moving the vessel to a nearby port for repairs. "That nice high tide came in and gave us a lot of lift and it made it very easy for the tugs to pull it off," said Petty Officer Mark Hunt, a Coast Guard spokesman. The vessel ran aground Saturday in Dundas Ba…

title: High toxin levels found in Alaskan killer whales
summary: High toxin levels found in Alaskan killer whales By DOUG O'HARRA Daily News reporter Though they live and feed in some of the world's most pristine seas, killer whales in Prince William Sound and the Kenai Fiords have been concentrating dangerous industrial pollutants of possible Asian origin in their body fat. Over the past five years, an ongoing scientific study has found PCBs, or polychlorinated biphenyls, and the pesticide DDT in 77 tiny samples of blubber taken from 74 of the area's whales. Such contaminants have been showing up in Alaska marine animals for years in trace amounts. But measurements of certain marine mammal-eating killer whales, called "transients," showed the poisonous chemicals at extremely high levels - some 14 to 22 times greater than among the fish-eating form of t…

title: Submersible to study Gulf of Alaska
summary: Submersible to study Gulf of Alaska Research to focus on undersea life, geology By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter Website: Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution On-line: www.marine.whoi.edu/ships/alvin/alvin.htm Scientists this summer will study underwater volcanoes, deep-water crabs and fish, and underwater methane leaks in the Gulf of Alaska using ALVIN, the same small research submarine used to scanthe wreck of the Titanic in 1986. The West Coast and Polar Regions Undersea Research Center at the University of Alaska Fairbanks is sponsoring the ALVIN Alaska Cruise 1999. It is scheduled to begin July 18 when its support ship, the 274-foot Atlantis, leaves Astoria, Ore. Researchers plan to make 21 dives once the submarine reaches Alaska waters. The scientific cruise is expected to end…

title: Snowmobilers lose part of park
summary: Snowmobilers lose part of park Temporary rules close Denali lands By CRAIG MEDRED Daily News outdoors editor The original Mount McKinley National Park has been temporarily closed to snowmobiles while the National Park Service considers a permanent ban, officials said Tuesday. Old McKinley park makes up about a third of today's Maryland-size Denali National Park and Preserve, said Denali spokeswoman Jane Tranel. The old park had been closed to snowmobiles and helicopters from its creation in 1917 until passage of the Alaska National Interest Lands Conservation Act in 1980, she added. ANILCA incorporated the old McKinley into the new, larger Denali. National Park Service officials for years thought the closure of the original portion of Denali remained in place after passage of ANILCA, said …

title: North Pole's recalled mayor keeps up fight
summary: North Pole's recalled mayor keeps up fight The Associated Press NORTH POLE - North Pole has a new mayor, but that doesn't mean the one ousted from office in a recall election last week is going to the door quietly. Tim Peters and nine other residents challenged the recall election on Monday, alleging that a "corrupt election practice" occurred in the days before Nov. 3 that was sufficient to change the outcome. Peters was removed from the $48,000-a-year job by a narrow margin, and on Monday the city council unanimously certified the results and swore in council member Johnny McCoy as temporary mayor until a special election takes place. That election will take place within 60 days. Recall organizers charged Peters with inadequate management of employees, mishandling a financial audit, wron…

title: Copyright © 1999 The Anchorage Daily News
summary: Grizzly seizes river No boating while bruin's in charge By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Daily News reporter A grizzly bear devouring a moose carcass on the bank of Eagle River forced two sets of canoeists out of the water over the weekend and caused Chugach State Park rangers to close boating access to the river. Then, as if to make sure the visitors knew they weren't welcome, the bear tried to trash their stuff. Chief park ranger Jerry Lewanski said the bear was dining right on the riverbank downstream from the canoe put-in point at Mile 7.4 of Eagle River Road, about six miles up the valley from town. Saturday, a couple canoeing the river tried to sneak around the bear but it charged, Lewanski said. They pulled their canoe to the opposite bank and walked out to the road. Sunday, another couple, Ma…

title: Eklutna fire culprit gets reprimand
summary: Eklutna fire culprit gets reprimand Homeowner may get bill for blaze-fighting efforts By NATALIE PHILLIPS Daily News reporter The state Division of Forestry has issued a written warning to the man who started the 380-acre fire that burned out of control last week in Eklutna Valley. And federal fire officials are looking at whether to send the Eklutna homeowner a bill for weeklong fire suppression efforts. More warnings and citations are expected to be issued to others who failed to control burns last week, according to Lynn Wilcock, state fire management officer for the Mat-Su area. A written warning doesn't involve any penalties or fines, but "is more of a public education thing," Wilcock said. "If another event arises, it will lead to criminal proceedings, possibly civil. The state has t…
